title: Governor
lead: Enshrined in ==Arts 153–167==, the Governor symbolises the link between Centre and States in India's quasi-federal structure. SC set assent timelines in [[State of TN v. Governor (2025)]] — later reversed via a ==Presidential Reference==.

items: Background: growing ==delays by Governors== in granting assent to Bills — sometimes causing ==legislative paralysis==.

==SC ruling (April 2025)== set explicit timelines under Art 200: ==1 month== to grant/withhold assent; ==3 months== if reserving for the President; ==1 month== for reconsidered Bills; and — for the first time — the ==President must decide referred Bills within 3 months==.
The SC ==reversed these timelines== later in 2025 while answering a ==Presidential Reference==.

head: Aspect
Position
Issues
Reform recommendations
rows: By the President (==Art 155==), effectively on Union advice.
Central dominance; ==reward for loyalty==; no consultation with states.
==Sarkaria==: eminent outsider, detached from politics; consult the CM · ==Punchhi==: fixed tenure, transparent panel-based selection · ==Rajamannar==: not the Centre's agent.
Minimal criteria; no bar on recent politicians or conflicts of interest.
Sarkaria & Punchhi: ==eminent persons only==, not active politicians; conventions: from outside the state.
==Pleasure of the President== (Art 156(1)) — no fixed term or reasons.
[[B.P. Singhal (2010)]]: removal cannot be arbitrary — needs ==valid, compelling reasons== · Punchhi: impeachment-like process · Rajamannar: state legislature involvement.
CM appointment in hung assemblies, ==reserving bills==, recommending Art 356.
Sarkaria: use ==sparingly==, judicial review · Punchhi: ==six-month timeline for assent== · II ARC: Inter-State Council guidelines.

items.title: Retain the Office?
bullets: Stability in crises — hung assemblies, breakdowns
Ensures states act ==within the constitutional framework==

items.title: Reform It
bullets: ==Misused for political purposes==, undermining state autonomy
Assent delays and discretionary interventions → ==legislative deadlock==
Rajamannar, Sarkaria, Punchhi all urge limits on central interference
Expert bodies recommend ==reform, not abolition==: fixed tenure, clear eligibility, transparent removal, limited discretion

sections.title: The Address to the Legislature — Arts 175 & 176

items: ==Core issue==: Governors skipping the annual address, refusing the cabinet-approved text, or ==omitting portions== — acute in Opposition-ruled states (Kerala, Karnataka).
==Art 176 uses ‘shall’== — the special address is a ==constitutional duty==, not a choice; Art 163 binds the Governor to ==aid and advice== except where discretion is expressly given.
[[Shamsher Singh v. Punjab]]: President and Governor act on ministerial advice outside specified discretionary areas.
[[Nabam Rebia]]: Art 175 dropped the 1935 Act's ==‘in his discretion’== phrase — messages must follow ministerial advice; the Governor cannot be a ==‘conscience keeper’== of the Assembly.
The Governor is a ==constitutional reader==, not an editor, of the government's policy statement — omitting Union-critical portions distorts ==responsible government==.

==Way forward==: prior consultation with the CM for genuine concerns; full cabinet-approved text laid on the table as ==official record==; codify the convention — refusal to deliver = serious breach of duty.
==Art 164==: Governor appoints the CM; discretion in a hung house is a ==temporary mechanism== to find who can face the House — not personal preference.

==Sarkaria–Punchhi order of invitation==: (1) ==pre-poll alliance==; (2) largest single party with support; (3) post-electoral coalition, all partners in government; (4) post-electoral alliance with outside support.

==Floor test is decisive==: [[SR Bommai (1994)]] — the Assembly, not Raj Bhavan arithmetic; [[Rameshwar Prasad (2006)]] — dissolution on feared horse-trading is anathema; [[Subhash Desai (2023)]] — the Governor's discretion to call a floor test is ==not unfettered==.
==Concerns==: inconsistent yardsticks (Goa, Manipur, Karnataka, Maharashtra); timing — ordinarily prove majority ==within 30 days==.

==TN-type issue==: the Governor may seek support letters as ==prima facie evidence== but cannot demand ==signed letters of an absolute majority before the oath== — minority governments are possible; ==Art 189== decides questions by majority of those present and voting.
==Guiding principle==: ‘Raj Bhavan may identify the claimant, but ==the Assembly alone certifies the majority==.’

blocks.text: The Governor was meant to be a sentinel of the Constitution, not a shadow of the Centre. When discretion turns into disruption, the balance of federalism falters. Restoring dignity to this office is not just a legal need — it is a democratic imperative.
